purpose: Section-specific generate prompt for authoring a principle's gates field (an array of { point, check, blocking } triples). Composed at runtime with system_prompts/generate.json.
system_addendum:
SECTION YOU ARE AUTHORING: gates

The gates field operationalises the solution. Where solution names the artefact and how it's wired into the lifecycle, gates names the specific check that fires at each lifecycle point, and whether failure blocks progression. Gates are what makes the principle audit-inspectable — a reader can point at the CI logs / branch protection / ARB review record and say 'the gate ran on this PR and either passed or blocked'.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD GATES SECTION

A principle's gates is an array of objects, each: { point: string, check: string, blocking: boolean }.

`point` is one of the lifecycle enums (pre_commit, pre_merge, pre_deploy, release, quarterly_review, annual_review, incident_response). Project-tier principles tend to gate at pre_merge and pre_deploy. Enterprise-tier principles tend to gate at release, quarterly_review, or annual_review.

`check` is a binary statement — something that either holds or doesn't, mechanically verifiable. Not 'the team should ensure X' but 'X exists at path Y and contains field Z'. For project-tier principles where the gate is enforced via CI, the check MUST explicitly state that it is configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy the gate. Without this branch-protection constraint in the check text, a PA can write a passing CI check that nobody is forced to wait for, and the gate quietly becomes optional.

`blocking` is a boolean — does failure prevent progression past the lifecycle point? Almost all CI-enforced gates in this catalogue are blocking: true. Non-blocking gates exist for advisory / monitoring purposes only and are rare.

Most principles in this catalogue have 1-3 gates. The common pattern is one existence/structure gate + one coupling gate (linking agent-code changes to changes in the principle's artefact). Solution-to-gates traceability is key: every artefact named in solution.approach should be enforced by at least one gate.

DIMENSIONS YOUR DRAFT WILL BE SCORED ON

Full rubric in sections/gates/rubric.json. The six dimensions:

1. lifecycle_point_valid — point is from the enums; appropriate for the principle's ownership tier.
2. check_is_binary_and_verifiable — check describes a mechanically verifiable condition, not a recommendation.
3. required_status_check_constraint_present — for project-tier CI-enforced gates, check text explicitly names the branch-protection constraint.
4. blocking_correctness — blocking boolean is correct (almost always true for CI-enforced project-tier).
5. coverage_completeness — gates collectively enforce every artefact named in solution.approach.
6. parallel_form_with_siblings — gate count (1-3), structure, branch-protection boilerplate match siblings.

All six must score ≥ 2 to pass.

COMMON FAILURE MODES TO AVOID

- check that reads as a recommendation ('the harness should be of high quality', 'PRs should be reviewed for X'). Not mechanically verifiable. Score low on check_is_binary_and_verifiable.
- Missing required-status-check constraint. Most common failure on first drafts. Score low on required_status_check_constraint_present. ALWAYS include the full branch-protection language verbatim for project-tier CI gates.
- Wrong lifecycle point. Enterprise-tier principles gating at pre_merge (when ARB doesn't see pre_merge), or project-tier gating at quarterly_review (when PA self-attests release-by-release). Score low on lifecycle_point_valid.
- Solution names two artefacts; gates only enforce one. Score low on coverage_completeness. Each solution.approach artefact needs gate coverage.
- Gate count outside 1-3 without strong reason. Score low on parallel_form_with_siblings.
user_template:
You are authoring the gates field for the principle with these properties:

<principle_id>{{ principle_id }}</principle_id>
<pillar>{{ pillar }}</pillar>
<focus_area>{{ focus_area }}</focus_area>
<ownership_tier>{{ ownership_tier }}</ownership_tier>

<aws_anchor>
  <best_practice>{{ aws_bp }}</best_practice>
  <implementation_step>
    <number>{{ aws_step_number }}</number>
    <title>{{ aws_step_title }}</title>
    <verbatim_text>{{ aws_step_verbatim }}</verbatim_text>
  </implementation_step>
</aws_anchor>

<statement>
  <title>{{ statement_title }}</title>
  <description>{{ statement_description }}</description>
</statement>

<problem>
  <description>{{ problem_description }}</description>
  <examples>{{ problem_examples }}</examples>
</problem>

<solution>
  <approach>{{ solution_approach }}</approach>
  <key_benefits>{{ solution_key_benefits }}</key_benefits>
</solution>

<siblings_under_same_bp>
{{ sibling_gates }}
</siblings_under_same_bp>

Draft the gates array for this principle. Return the JSON object specified in the output contract.

schema.gates: array of { point: string, check: string, blocking: boolean } — typically 1-3 items

gates.check: On any PR, both `eval/scenarios/` and `eval/data/` must exist in the repository and be non-empty. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR that touches files under `src/agent/`, the test harness in `eval/` must run against the proposed changes and all tests must pass. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge and stalls migration to production.
